content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10) – the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 10^9).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line – the number of divisors of n that are lucky numbers (numbers whose decimal representation consists only of digits 4 and 7).

sampleTestCases.explanation: Lucky numbers ≤10 are 4 and 7. For each n we count how many of these divide n. Only 4 divides 4, 7 divides 7, and 4 divides 8, giving a count of 1 for those cases; all other numbers have no lucky divisor.

solution.approach: All lucky numbers not larger than 10^9 are generated once by a depth‑first recursion that appends digit 4 or 7. Their total count is only about 2^10 ≈ 1022, so they can be stored in a vector/array. For each query n we simply iterate over the pre‑computed lucky numbers (stopping when the lucky number exceeds n) and count those that divide n. This yields O(L) work per test case where L is the number of lucky numbers (≈1022), easily fast for T ≤ 10.
solution.timeComplexity: Precomputation O(L) where L ≈ 1022. Each test case O(L) in the worst case, so total O(T·L) ≤ 10·1022 ≈ 1e4 operations.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(L) to store all lucky numbers (≈1022 64‑bit integers).
